data.title: Bernie Sanders, Theo Von bash parties for not working for people
data.description:
Theo Von isn’t your typical political gatekeeper. He’s a comedian, a podcaster and whether he set out to be or not, he is one of the most influential conversationalists in today’s MAGA-friendly media ecosystem. But what makes him interesting isn’t that he leans right or left. It’s that he doesn’t fully belong to either side.  

He said it plainly on a recent episode of “This Past Weekend”: “It doesn’t even feel like either one of these parties works for anybody anymore.”  

That line reflects how a lot of Americans feel right now … politically homeless. And the numbers back it up. A Gallup poll shows a record-high 45 percent of U.S. adults identified as political independents in 2025 — nearly half the country. That’s not apathy, that’s disillusionment.  

Theo’s guest this week was independent Sen. Bernie Sanders, someone who knows what it means to operate outside traditional party lines. Bernie made it clear that starting a new party isn’t realistic right now, which is why he caucuses with Democrats even as he argues the party itself is split.  

“We are trying to move the Democratic Party to be a party that stands with working-class Americans and has the courage to take on powerful special interests,” said Sanders. “Now there’s another faction of the Democratic Party that, you know, doesn’t like Trump, for I think a lot of right reasons, I agree, but basically cuddles up to the moneyed interests. And that’s the vision that we have.”  

That divide matters, because it helps explain why so many voters don’t feel represented by either side.  

The conversation turned to immigration and ICE, another issue where public opinion is shifting. A CBS-YouGov poll found that 61 percent of Americans now describe ICE as being “too tough.” More Americans believe enforcement efforts are sweeping up people beyond dangerous criminals, including those who’ve lived here for years, worked and raised families.  

Bernie laid out what he believes most Americans actually agree on. “If you have lived in this, even if you came across the border illegally, but if you have obeyed the rules of America, you haven’t committed crimes, you’re working hard, you’re raising your family, you need a path towards citizenship as part of comprehensive immigration reform. And, by the way, given the fact that we have worker shortages all over this country, we need those workers.” 

Then he went further, criticizing how President Trump is using ICE now.  

“I think it’s Trump saying, ‘I have the power and we got a domestic army. It’s called ICE. And we’re going to go out there and we’re going to arrest and we’re going to intimidate.’ And part of that, this is what Trumpism is about. It’s intimidation … I really that scares me because that’s what authoritarianism is about.”  

What makes this moment notable isn’t Bernie Sanders saying it, it’s where he’s saying it. On Theo Von’s podcast — a space that doesn’t fit neatly into left or right, but reaches millions who are skeptical of both.  

What this moment shows isn’t a shift toward the left or the right  but a demand for accountability. Voters aren’t asking for purity tests or party loyalty. They’re asking for policies that reflect how people actually live, work and raise families. When nearly half the country identifies as independent, and majorities say enforcement has gone too far, the message is clear: the old political playbook isn’t matching public reality.   

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Former police officer, 2020 election denier get into altercation at Jack Smith hearing
data.description:
Former D.C. Metropolitan Police Officer Michael Fanone, who defended the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021, and Ivan Raiklin, a conservative activist and conspiracy theorist who frequently appears on Capitol Hill to protest the outcome of the 2020 election, got into an altercation Thursday at a hearing featuring former special counsel Jack Smith.

Video of the confrontation shows Fanone and Raiklin facing off during a short break in Smith’s testimony as other former police officers in attendance try to pull Fanone back.

“See how many people are restraining you, and look at me, totally in control over my mind and body,” Raiklin says to Fanone.

A former Capitol Police Officer who also served on Jan. 6, Harry Dunn, tries to pull Fanone away and uniformed officers step between the two.

“This guy has threatened my family, threatened my children, threatened to rape my children, you sick bastard,” Fanone says, pointing at Raiklin.

In response to the clash, more U.S. Capitol Police officers were called to the hearing room to keep the peace, some of whom spoke with Raiklin. 

A short time later, Raiklin left the hearing room, though it’s unclear if he was ordered to do so or left on his own accord.

Raiklin was not the only figure connected to Jan. 6 conspiracies in attendance Thursday. Oath Keepers Founder Stewart Rhodes was also at the hearing.

Rhodes was convicted of seditious conspiracy and sentenced to 18 years in prison for his actions connected to Jan. 6, but President Trump commuted his sentence as one of his first actions after being inaugurated last January.

Smith, who as special counsel led probes into Trump for his efforts to overturn the 2020 election and mishandling of classified documents, is testifying publicly before the House Judiciary Committee. Both his cases against Trump were dropped when the president returned to power.

NewsNation’s Joe Khalil contributed.

data.title: So cold that trees will explode? Not exactly, expert explains
data.description:
(WFRV) – It’s about to be very cold in parts of the U.S., including the Midwest, where arctic air and strong winds could drop the feel-like temperatures into the -40s.

These forecasts have prompted warnings about protecting your water pipes, dressing for the weather, and, for some reason, the possibility of “exploding” trees.

Earlier this week, posts shared to social media began spreading news of the alleged phenomenon, and warning of impending doom to backyard birches, forests of firs, and every tree between.

While you likely won’t witness a tree erupting at its trunk, hurling splinters and branches every which way, you may hear your trees responding to the frigid temperatures.

As anyone familiar with tapping trees for syrup can attest, sap within a tree can become quite solid during the winter, but it may not freeze as sap freezes at temperatures colder than water. So when it does get cold enough for the sap to freeze, there can be a very sudden physical expansion which can sometimes lead to a crack or branches splitting, a forest health specialist with the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources (DNR) told Nexstar’s WFRV.

The specialist, though, said he has never seen a tree “explode” due to cold temperatures, as it is extremely rare.

Nonetheless, you may hear a loud sound coming from a tree during frigid days. If you notice that a tree in your yard has cracked or a branch has split off, the DNR recommends consulting an expert to ensure the safety of your property or the tree.

The specialist noted that some trees may be more prone to impacts of cold weather based on rainfall, or lack thereof. For example, parts of southern Wisconsin had record rainfall last year, which could cause trees to hold more water, and potentially fall victim to the sub-freezing temperatures. Trees in the northern part of the state experienced less rainfall in 2025, which could make them less likely to crack.

data.title: Watch live: Health insurance CEOs testify before House on rising premiums
data.description:
The House Ways and Means Committee will hear testimony Thursday afternoon from a group of health insurance CEOs as premiums skyrocket following the expiration of Affordable Care Act subsidies.

Among the list of witnesses are UnitedHealth Group’s Stephen Hemsley, CVS Health’s David Joyner, Elevance Health’s Gail Boudreaux, Cigna Group’s David Cordani, Ascendiun’s Paul Markovich and TnK Health and Nutrition’s ReShonda Young.

Several of the CEOs also faced questioning before the House Energy and Commerce Committee earlier in the day.

The subsidies — which helped keep insurance costs low for struggling Americans — were at the heart of the record 43-day lapse in government funding last year. Lawmakers are now facing a partial shutdown deadline, and Democrats have considered withholding support for spending bills if the issue isn’t resolved.

The hearing is scheduled to begin at 2:30 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Why major US allies aren't joining Trump's 'Board of Peace' yet
data.description:
Several key U.S. allies were notably not present at President Trump’s “Board of Peace” charter-signing ceremony in Davos, Switzerland, on Thursday. 

The international organization, which will be headed by Trump, aims to promote cooperation around the globe and carry out the Trump administration’s 20-point Gaza peace plan. Trump sent invitations to leaders of a wide range of nations last week to join the board. 

The countries who have agreed to join the board thus far are Argentina, Belarus, Morocco, Vietnam, Pakistan, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kosovo, Hungary, Egypt, Turkey, Qatar, Jordan, Indonesia and Saudi Arabia. 

Several European countries who did not sign on to the agreement pointed to delays with parliamentary approval processes required before signing on to international agreements. 

Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, cited potential legal risks associated with signing the agreement, telling Italian state media on Wednesday, “Some elements are incompatible with our constitution.”

However, the long-time Trump ally said, “My position is still one of openness.”

Similarly, Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk wrote in a post on the social platform X on Monday that the country’s laws require approval of international organization membership from Poland’s two parliamentary houses, the Council of Ministers and the Sejm. 

“The government will be guided solely by the interest and security of the Polish state,” Tusk wrote. “And we will not let anyone play us.”

Germany expressed interest in joining the board but has not officially accepted the invitation. 

A government spokesperson announced at a press conference that German Chancellor Friedrich Merz could not make the signing ceremony due to planned travel and emphasized the continued importance of the United Nations in resolving international disputes, Politico reported. 

Japan’s chief cabinet secretary said on Wednesday the country is “examining the details,” according to local media reports. The spokesperson also reportedly said the country plans to “strongly support” the board’s peace efforts in the Middle East.  

The South Korean government is also reviewing its invitation to join the board. 

“The government welcomes the Trump administration’s initiative and is actively reviewing whether to join,” a senior Korean government official told local media on Thursday. “Some believe joining sooner rather than later would be preferable, and the government is unlikely to delay once it settles on a direction.”

The United Kingdom, however, cited particular concerns with the board invitation sent by the Trump administration to Russian President Vladimir Putin amid Russia’s ongoing war with Ukraine. 

British Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper noted that the U.K. is still committed to assisting the Board of Peace’s efforts to rebuild Gaza regardless of their membership status on the board.  

“We have seen no sign that Putin is actually willing to come and make that agreement,” Cooper told BBC Breakfast on Thursday. “That’s where the pressure needs to be now.”

Additionally, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney questioned where the hefty $1 billion fee tied to becoming a permanent board member will go. A three-year membership carries no such financial obligation. 

Carney told reporters on Sunday that his country “wants money to have maximum impact.”

“We still do not have unimpeded aid flows, humanitarian aid flows at scale to the people in Gaza. … That is a precondition for moving forward on this,” he said. 

The refusal of some of America’s allies to join the agreement has sparked strong negative reactions from the U.S. president.

After learning that French President Emmanuel Macron had shot down the prospect of immediately signing on to the board, Trump threatened to impose 200 percent tariffs on the country’s champagne and wine exports to the U.S. 

At the World Economic Forum in Davos on Tuesday, the French president accused his U.S. counterpart of attempting to “weaken and subordinate” Europe after Trump threatened to seize Greenland and tax imports from several Danish allies. 

Trump has since walked back these threats, saying on Wednesday he will not use military force to acquire Greenland and will not roll out the planned tariffs. 

Additionally, Norway’s decision not to join the board followed a dispute between the country’s prime minister and Trump over the U.S. bid for Greenland and the president’s attempts to secure the Nobel Peace Prize. 

In a letter to Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre, Trump accused the country of denying him the prize and said, “I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace.”

While the Nobel Committee responsible for selecting the Nobel Peace Prize recipient is based in Norway, Støre stressed the government has no role in this selection process. 

“I have clearly explained, including to president Trump what is well known, the prize is awarded by an independent Nobel Committee and not the Norwegian Government,” he wrote in a Monday statement.  

State Secretary Kristoffer Thoner wrote in a statement shared with The Straits Times on Wednesday that Trump’s board offer “raises a number of questions” that necessitate “further dialogue with the United States.”

“Norway will therefore not join the proposed arrangements for the Board of Peace, and will therefore not attend a signing ceremony in Davos,” Thoner said.

data.title: Scott Jennings calls out Cameron Kasky over false Trump-Epstein smear
data.description:
CNN’s Scott Jennings is spitting fire, objecting to bald-faced lies spouted by one of his fellow panelists.  

We already discussed some of the clips from that recent episode, but I was gobsmacked when I saw this one. Cameron Kasky, a progressive commentator — and, briefly, a Democratic candidate for the House — accused President Trump of being part of a sex trafficking ring. Jennings rightly called him out for uttering such an absurd lie. 

This is, of course, false. Yes, Trump was provably an acquaintance of Jeffrey Epstein, a sexual predator, in the ’90s and early 2000s, just like Bill Clinton and a whole host of other famous people. Trump’s association with Epstein allegedly ended before the sex crimes came to light, and no one has presented a shred of evidence that Trump was involved in the sex crimes. Nor have they proven that Clinton was, either.  

This is where the discourse is at surrounding Epstein: Anyone who has met him even once can be smeared as “very involved” in a sex trafficking ring on CNN.  

To be clear, the network is not responsible for the crazy things its guests say — they should have on a range of perspectives, of course. I expect, however, that they would not invite to sit on one of those panels a 9/11 truther, a Stop the Steal obsessive or a Pizzagate conspiracy theorist. Pizzagate, you may recall, was an online social media conspiracy promoted by fringe far-right personalities that there was an underground child sex ring at a pizza place in Washington, D.C., and it was somehow connected to the Clintons and other high-profile Democrats.

Responsible mainstream journalists worked to debunk that conspiracy theory; they did not invite its adherents to become regular commentators on CNN. How times have changed. 

In fairness to Kasky, the Epstein sex trafficking ring was real, it has real victims, and it is right to keep seeking answers about who else was involved. I suppose efforts by Thomas Massie, Ro  Khanna and others to release all government files related to Epstein, and I’ve criticized the administration for moving slowly on that. 

But you just can’t say that Trump is provably “very involved” in a sex trafficking ring. It’s totally false and made up—the kind of vicious smear, in fact, that discredits good-faith efforts to expose Epstein and fellow abusers. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Klobuchar takes step toward Minnesota governor bid
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) filed to form a gubernatorial committee on Thursday, putting her one step closer toward a run for Minnesota governor. 

Klobuchar filed paperwork with the Minnesota Campaign Finance and Public Disclosure Board for a gubernatorial committee. A spokesperson for Klobuchar’s campaign did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Klobuchar is widely expected to announce a run for governor after Gov. Tim Walz (D) said earlier this month he would be dropping his bid for a third term. Klobuchar has confirmed publicly that’s she considering a run.

A Klobuchar gubernatorial bid would be a boost for Democrats, who are thrilled at the prospect of her running. Walz has been mired in negative headlines over a statewide fraud scandal, the political ramifications of which ultimately led him to drop out.

Meanwhile, a host of Republicans are running for the GOP nod in the Minnesota governor’s race, including Minnesota House Speaker Lisa Demuth (R), MyPillow CEO and Trump loyalist Mike Lindell, former GOP gubernatorial candidate Kendall Qualls and state Reps. Kristin Robbins and Peggy Bennett, among others.

While Republicans have made the issue of fraud a key issue in the 2026 midterms, Republicans also face an uphill battle in flipping the governor’s seat as the GOP hasn’t won statewide since 2006 when former Gov. Tim Pawlenty (R) won reelection.

data.title: Blue Origin launches 17th crewed mission to space: Who was on it?
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Blue Origin sent six more people to the edge of space on its New Shepard rocket on Thursday morning.

The launch, dubbed Mission NS-38, lifted off from the company’s West Texas launch site at 11:25 a.m. EST. The crew reached their apogee in the spacecraft mere moments later, before returning back to Earth by approximately 11:36 a.m.

The crew of Mission NS-38 included: Tim Drexler, a pilot and the CEO of Ace Asphalt; Dr. Linda Edwards, an avid traveler and retired obstetrician/gynecologist; Alain Fernandez, a real estate developer and former scuba instructor; Alberto Gutiérrez, an entrepreneur who founded the travel experiences company Civitatis; and Jim Hendron, a retired Air Force colonel and the founder of Hendren Plastics.

Earlier this week, Blue Origin also announced that Laura Stiles, the director of New Shepard Launch Operations for the space company, was launching into space with the crew of NS-38, replacing a previously announced passenger who dropped out “due to illness.”

“Laura is also a skydiving instructor and holds 2 World Records in large-formation skydiving,” Blue Origin wrote of Stiles in a recent blog entry. “She recently began her journey toward becoming a fixed-wing pilot.”

Thursday’s launch was the 17th crewed Blue Origin flight to ferry passengers into space. The previous mission, NS-37, was successfully completed just over a month ago on Dec. 20.

Blue Origin often boasts that its New Shepard spacecraft brings its crew past the Kármán line, which is recognized as the boundary between Earth’s atmosphere and space by the Fédération Aéronautique Internationale, a Swiss-based governing body and record-keeping organization. For years, the space company has also maintained that the Kármán Line is the threshold that separates Earth’s atmosphere from space.

In 2021, Blue Origin made this opinion known when Virgin Group founder Richard Branson successfully rocketed to an altitude of approximately 282,000 feet, or over 53 miles, on a Virgin Galactic space plane called the VSS Unity. At the time, the company further suggested that Branson and his fellow astronauts would need “asterisks” next to their names.

“From the beginning, New Shepard was designed to fly above the Kármán line, so none of our astronauts have an asterisk next to their name,” the company wrote on social platform X at the time. “For 96% of the world’s population, space begins 100 km up at the internationally recognized Kármán line.”

The boundary between Earth and “space,” meanwhile, is not clearly defined. Astrophysicist Jonathan McDowell has proposed the boundary should be lower, at around 80 kilometers up, arguing that satellites can survive certain elliptical orbits that dip to this height. But NASA heliophysicist Doug Rowland said it’s tough to demarcate where “space” begins, because Earth’s atmosphere doesn’t necessarily stop at any single point, but instead “just gets less and less dense the higher you go.”

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) further pointed out that a spacecraft would have to travel 600 miles above Earth to escape the planet’s atmosphere completely — meaning that the International Space Station (orbiting between 205 and 270 miles up) wouldn’t even be considered as being in proper “space,” either.

“When you go to where the Space Station is — only a couple of hundred miles above the Earth — there’s still enough air there to slow the Space Station down. And if you didn’t re-boost it with rockets, it would come back to Earth based on the air drag,” Rowland said.

data.title: Trump sues JPMorgan Chase, Jamie Dimon for $5B over alleged 'debanking'
data.description:
President Trump sued JPMorgan Chase and its longtime CEO, Jamie Dimon, on Thursday over accusations the company “debanked” him by closing his accounts in 2021 and putting him on a blacklist. 

The 26-page complaint, filed in Florida state court in Miami, seeks at least $5 billion in damages from JPMorgan Chase (JPMC), the largest U.S. bank by assets. 

“Plaintiffs are confident that JPMC’s unilateral decision came about as a result of political and social motivations, and JPMC’s unsubstantiated, ‘woke’ beliefs that it needed to distance itself from President Trump and his conservative political views,” the lawsuit states.  

“In essence, JPMC debanked Plaintiffs’ Accounts because it believed that the political tide at the moment favored doing so,” it continued. 

Trump and Republicans have long accused big banks of discriminating against conservatives.

Last summer, the president signed an executive order directing regulators to investigate and punish financial institutions for “politicized or unlawful debanking.” And he has repeatedly spoken about the issue publicly, including his speech to last year’s World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. 

His new lawsuit centers on JP Morgan Chase’s decision to close several bank accounts affiliated with Trump in Feb. 2021, weeks after the Jan. 6 Capitol attack. At the time, Trump faced an increasingly challenging financial future as he left office and was on the hook for hundreds of millions of dollars of debt.

Alejandro Brito, Trump’s attorney, wrote in the lawsuit that JPMorgan Chase’s decision caused Trump “overwhelming financial and reputational harm.” He said the bank did not provide a reason, and it was really about political discrimination. 

“While we regret President Trump has sued us, we believe the suit has no merit,” JP Morgan Chase said in a statement. “We respect the President’s right to sue us and our right to defend ourselves – that’s what courts are for.” 

The company maintained it “does not close accounts for political or religious reasons,” saying it does so because they create legal or regulatory risk.

“We regret having to do so but often rules and regulatory expectations lead us to do so,” the statement continued. “We have been asking both this Administration and prior administrations to change the rules and regulations that put us in this position, and we support the Administration’s efforts to prevent the weaponization of the banking sector.” 

The suit lands days after Trump threatened the bank with legal action. In a Truth Social post on Saturday, the president mentioned he’d be bringing the suit as he denied reporting from The Wall Street Journal that he months ago offered Dimon the job of Federal Reserve chair. 

JPMorgan Chase becomes the latest major corporation to face a lawsuit from Trump in his personal capacity. The list has largely comprised media companies.

Just before Trump took office for his second term, ABC News settled a defamation lawsuit for $15 million. Paramount later paid $16 million to settle Trump’s lawsuit over editing in a “60 Minutes” interview with then-Vice President Harris. 

And Trump has filed lawsuits that remain pending against other entities that include The Wall Street Journal, the BBC and The New York Times.

data.title: Homicide rate falls in dozens of cities: Research
data.description:
The homicide rate across 35 major cities fell dramatically in 2025, dropping to what researchers say could be the lowest rate on record, a new study from the Council on Criminal Justice (CCJ) found.

Homicides are down 21 percent from 2024 and 44 percent from a recent peak in 2021, according to the CCJ report, which analyzes crime data from 40 large American cities, including 35 which report full data for the deaths.

The average homicide rate in the 35 cities was 10.4 in 2025, down from 13.2 in 2024, 15.9 in 2023 and 17.7 in 2022. The rate peaked at 18.6 in 2021, up from 18 in 2020, 13.9 in 2019 and 12.9 in 2018, per the study. Homicide rates are measured per 100,000 residents.  

When the FBI, later this year, reports 2025 crime data for jurisdictions of all sizes, according to the CCJ analysis, “there is a strong possibility that homicide in 2025 will drop to about 4.0 per 100,000 residents.” 

“That would mark the largest single-year percentage drop in the homicide rate on record — and be the lowest rate recorded in law enforcement or public health data going back to 1900,” the authors wrote.

From 2024 to 2025, homicide rates fell in 31 of the 35 major cities included in the study, for an average decline of 21 percent. 

The largest drop in homicide rate came from Denver, Washington, D.C., and Omaha, Neb. — which reported declines of 41 percent, 40 percent and 40 percent, respectively. Another 7 cities saw drops of over 30 percent: Los Angeles, Buffalo, N.Y., Albuquerque, N.M., Long Beach, Calif., Atlanta, Baltimore and Chicago, according to the study.

Only three cities saw homicides increase: Little Rock, Ark., by 16 percent; Fort Worth, Texas, by 2 percent; and Milwaukee, Wis., by 1 percent. El Paso, Texas saw no change in homicide rate. 

The analysis refrained from drawing any conclusions about what is driving the historic decline in homicides and cautioned against making assumptions without “robust research” measuring causal effects. 

“We’re seeing big swings in criminal justice policies, programs, and rhetoric, big advances in crime-fighting technologies, and big social, economic, and cultural shifts all happening at the same time. It’s extremely difficult to disentangle and pinpoint what’s actually driving the drop,” CCJ president and CEO Adam Gelb said in a statement. 

“As a result, we have a battle of sound bites and abundant claims of credit but scarce hard evidence to back them up,” Gelb added.
